does: This paper establishes a theoretical framework explaining why LLMs optimized via outcome-based reinforcement learning develop brittle reasoning that fails out-of-distribution. The authors integrate Structural Causal Models with the Information Bottleneck principle to formalize the phenomenon of Reward-Induced Manifold Collapse, where models exploit low-complexity spurious correlations rather than learning genuine causal reasoning. They prove that under SGD's implicit inductive bias, outcome-optimized models are systematically biased toward shortcuts whenever training data permits Markovian screening of true causal mechanisms. The paper derives a new generalization bound based on Semantic Coverage Measure rather than sample size, demonstrating that scaling data on homogeneous distributions cannot correct underlying reasoning deficits. This provides formal justification for empirically observed gaps between in-distribution performance and out-of-distribution capability.
connects: This work responds to long-standing observations that neural networks exploit spurious correlations and shortcut learning, extending classical work on the information bottleneck and causal reasoning in machine learning. It builds on recent empirical findings showing LLMs fail on distribution shift despite strong benchmark performance, connecting to debates around process versus outcome rewards in RL-aligned systems. The theoretical contribution integrates causal inference frameworks with information-theoretic principles, synthesizing insights from structural causal models, simplicity bias in SGD, and the failure modes documented in adversarial robustness and OOD generalization literature. The work extends information-theoretic bounds in learning theory by introducing semantic coverage rather than sample complexity as the fundamental limiting factor for reasoning tasks.
enables: This framework enables more principled design of alignment procedures that move beyond outcome optimization alone. The theoretical results motivate why process reward models, which directly supervise reasoning steps rather than outcomes, could circumvent Markovian screening and enforce genuine causal learning. The Semantic Coverage Measure provides a diagnostic tool for assessing whether datasets enable shortcut learning, potentially guiding data curation and curriculum design. Future work could develop training procedures that explicitly prevent manifold collapse through information-theoretic regularization or causal constraints. The framework also enables better understanding of when and why scaling helps versus hurts reasoning capability, suggesting investigation into intervention-based training approaches and the design of intrinsically non-Markovian learning objectives that force models to recover full causal structure.
